subject: Blog post request: Pattern Generators — What Your Brain's Walking Circuits Teach Us About AI Architecture

body.summary: Blog post about applying central pattern generator (CPG) neuroscience to AI cognitive architecture. 17 principles mapped from biological CPGs to agent design. Five-stage crystallization pipeline. Adaptive forgetting. Three-discipline evaluation validated by 30+ literature sources. This represents the project's clearest expression of psychology-first engineering.

blog_brief.proposed_title: Pattern Generators: What Your Brain's Walking Circuits Teach Us About AI Architecture
blog_brief.source_material: ideas.md §CPG-Inspired Pattern Generators, journal.md §58, docs/cogarch-refactor-evaluation.md, docs/trigger-tiering-classification.md, docs/working-memory-spec.md
key_references: Graham Brown (1911) — half-center model for CPGs
Sumers et al. (2023) — CoALA framework for language agents

voter.angle: Your brain has circuits that make you walk without you thinking about it. These circuits — called central pattern generators — produce rhythmic movements automatically, adjusting to terrain without conscious effort. We applied the same idea to AI: what if an AI system could maintain itself the way your brain maintains your walking? The result: 17 design principles drawn from neuroscience that tell an AI system when to check its own work, when to switch between creative and critical thinking, and when to forget patterns that no longer serve it. The basic science drives the engineering, not the other way around.
voter.hook: You've walked thousands of miles without thinking about it. What if AI could maintain itself the same way?
voter.key_takeaway: The brain already solved the problem of self-maintaining systems. We borrowed its answers.

politician.angle: AI governance frameworks (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF) require transparent, auditable autonomous systems. This work demonstrates a principled approach: grounding AI agent behavior in established neuroscience rather than ad-hoc engineering. The result — a tiered enforcement system where safety-critical checks always run while lower-priority checks sample efficiently — reduces AI system overhead by 31-72% while maintaining safety guarantees. The three-discipline evaluation (engineering, law, psychology) found that the architecture already exceeds 92% of organizations' auditability standards (McKinsey, 2025).
politician.hook: 92% of organizations lack auditability for AI agent decisions. This architecture addresses that gap.
politician.key_takeaway: Psychology-grounded AI architecture produces governance compliance as a side effect of good design, not as bolted-on regulation.

educator.angle: Walk through the crystallization pipeline — how an AI system's cognitive patterns mature from fluid experimentation to stable infrastructure, mirroring human skill acquisition (Fitts & Posner, 1967). Stage 0: an idea in a notebook. Stage 1: the AI explicitly reasons through the pattern each time. Stage 2: the pattern encodes as a trigger that fires automatically on a condition. Stage 3: the pattern runs as a script without consuming the AI's attention. Stage 4: the pattern operates as infrastructure the AI doesn't participate in at all. Each stage reduces cognitive cost while increasing reliability — exactly how humans learn to drive, type, or play an instrument.
educator.hook: Remember learning to drive? First you thought about every gear shift. Now you don't think about driving at all. AI systems can learn the same way.
educator.key_takeaway: The crystallization pipeline gives AI systems a developmental trajectory — they don't just configure, they mature.

researcher.angle: 17 CPG principles mapped via analogical transfer from neuroscience to software architecture, each with 2x knock-on analysis (orders 1-10). Validated against CoALA (Sumers et al., 2023/TMLR 2024), MAP (Nature Communications, 2025), LIDA (Franklin, 2007), and ACT-R (Anderson, 2007). Analogical transfer risk flagged throughout — biological properties may not transfer to software agents. The crystallization pipeline draws from Fitts & Posner (1967), Anderson's ACT-R (1982), and Dreyfus & Dreyfus (1980). Adaptive forgetting grounded in Huttenlocher (1979) synaptic pruning and Ebbinghaus (1885) forgetting curve. Three-discipline evaluation produced 55 findings: 3 critical defects (all fixed), 39 structural weaknesses (tiering applied), 7 gaps (specs created), 4 dead weight items (removed).
researcher.hook: We mapped 17 neuroscience principles to AI architecture, traced 10-order knock-on effects for each, and validated against 30+ literature sources from 2023-2025.
researcher.key_takeaway: Analogical transfer from neuroscience to AI architecture produces testable design hypotheses — not metaphor, but principled engineering grounded in established science.
epistemic_flags: All 17 principles derive from analogical reasoning — transfer risk from biological circuits to software agents present throughout
The crystallization pipeline lacks empirical validation in AI systems — theoretical grounding only
Literature validation used web search and abstracts — not all papers read in full
The cogarch evaluation was conducted by the system being evaluated — circular evaluation risk mitigated by external literature grounding

developer.angle: Psychology and neuroscience provide our only reference point for intelligence as we know it. Making use of established patterns from these disciplines gives AI architecture principled grounding that pure engineering lacks. Concrete deliverables: trigger tiering (93 checks → 34 critical / 43 advisory / 16 spot-check, 31-72% overhead reduction), behavioral modes (generate/evaluate competition with fatigue-based switching), metacognitive tracking (trigger_activations table enables 'which checks actually catch errors?'), work completion pattern generator (tracks carryover across sessions), five-stage crystallization pipeline (concept → infrastructure), and a memory ownership contract with ACT-R-inspired activation scoring for retrieval priority.
developer.hook: We rebuilt our AI's cognitive architecture using the brain as a blueprint. Here's what 17 neuroscience principles look like as working code.
developer.key_takeaway: The brain and human psychology remain our only reference for intelligence. Ignoring that reference means reinventing solutions that neuroscience already discovered.
implementation_highlights: Trigger tiering: ⬛ CRITICAL (always, hook-backed) / ▣ ADVISORY (context-gated) / ▢ SPOT-CHECK (sampled)
CPG modes: Generative / Evaluative / Neutral with fatigue-based switching after 5 responses
Metacognitive SQL: SELECT trigger_id, catch_rate FROM trigger_activations GROUP BY trigger_id
Work carryover: dual_write.py work-carryover tracks unfinished items across sessions
Crystallization: Stage 0 (idea) → Stage 1 (reasoning) → Stage 2 (trigger) → Stage 3 (hook) → Stage 4 (daemon)

action_gate.gate_condition: unratified-agent drafts, reviews, and publishes the blog post

epistemic_flags: Blog post source material exists entirely within the psychology-agent project — unratified-agent has not independently verified the claims
The 30+ literature sources were gathered via web search — publication dates verified, full papers not read in all cases
Analogical transfer risk (neuroscience → software) flagged throughout the source material and should persist in the published post
